questions.q: What decisive new rule did Einstein add to Planck's photon idea to explain the photoelectric effect?
options: An electron can absorb energy from many photons and add it up over time
A single photon collides with a single electron and transfers all of its energy at once, in one interaction
Photons travel slower in metals, giving electrons time to escape
Light waves push electrons out by the pressure of their amplitude
questions.explanation: Einstein's master key is the one-photon-one-electron collision: the whole photon energy is delivered in a single hit, all or nothing. This explains the no-delay and threshold observations the wave model could not.

questions.q: Which equation correctly states the maximum kinetic energy of a photoelectron?
options: \(K_{\max} = hf + \phi\)
\(K_{\max} = hf - \phi\), where \(\phi\) is the work function
\(K_{\max} = \phi - hf\)
\(K_{\max} = hf \times \phi\)
questions.explanation: By conservation of energy, photon energy = work function + kinetic energy, so \(K_{\max} = hf - \phi\). The photon energy in is shared between escaping the metal (\(\phi\)) and the leftover kinetic energy.

questions.q: What is the work function of a metal?
options: The maximum kinetic energy a photoelectron can have
The minimum energy required to liberate an electron from the metal's surface
The frequency of light that ejects the fastest electrons
The total energy stored in the metal's electrons
questions.explanation: The work function \(\phi\) (or \(W\)) is the minimum energy needed to free a surface electron. It is an energy (joules or eV), not a frequency — quoting it in hertz is a classic error.

questions.q: On a graph of maximum kinetic energy versus frequency, what does each feature represent?
options: Gradient = work function; x-intercept = Planck's constant; y-intercept = threshold frequency
Gradient = Planck's constant; x-intercept = threshold frequency; y-intercept = negative work function
Gradient = threshold frequency; x-intercept = work function; y-intercept = Planck's constant
Gradient = negative work function; x-intercept = Planck's constant; y-intercept = threshold frequency
questions.explanation: Comparing \(K_{\max} = hf - \phi\) with \(y = mx + c\): the gradient is \(h\), the x-intercept (where \(K_{\max}=0\)) is the threshold frequency, and the y-intercept is \(-\phi\). Remember 'GIN'.

questions.q: Two metals X and Y are plotted on a \(K_{\max}\) vs frequency graph, with Y having a larger work function. How do the lines compare?
options: Y's line is steeper because a larger work function increases the gradient
The lines are parallel (same gradient = Planck's constant); Y's line is shifted right, with a higher threshold frequency and a more negative y-intercept
The lines cross at the threshold frequency
Y's line is shallower and shifted left
questions.explanation: The gradient is Planck's constant — a universal constant identical for every metal — so all photoelectric lines are parallel. A larger work function shifts the line right (higher threshold) and down (more negative intercept). Converging or differently-sloped lines are the headline error.

questions.q: A metal has a work function of 1.67 eV. Light of photon energy 2.92 eV strikes it. What is the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons, and the stopping voltage?
questions.explanation: \(K_{\max} = hf - \phi = 2.92 - 1.67 = 1.25\,\mathrm{eV}\). Since \(K_{\max} = eV_0\) and energy is in eV, the stopping voltage reads straight across as 1.25 V.

questions.q: Why does the photoelectric effect have a threshold frequency, in Einstein's model?
options: Below a certain brightness, no photons reach the metal
If a single photon's energy \(hf\) is less than the work function, no individual photon can free an electron, no matter how many arrive
The metal cools below the temperature needed to release electrons
Low-frequency photons are reflected by the metal surface
questions.explanation: Because an electron can only take energy from one photon at a time, if each photon's \(hf\) is below \(\phi\), none can free an electron — regardless of intensity or duration. The threshold is where \(hf_0 = \phi\), so \(f_0 = \phi/h\).

questions.q: How did Millikan's work validate the quantum model of light?
options: He proved light is purely a wave by measuring interference
He measured stopping voltage versus frequency for alkali metals, got straight parallel lines whose gradient equalled Planck's constant — the same value found independently from black-body radiation
He showed photons have mass and charge
He invented the photomultiplier tube to count single photons
questions.explanation: Millikan set out to disprove Einstein but instead confirmed the linear prediction, with gradient equal to Planck's constant. That same \(h\) had already been found from black-body radiation — two unrelated experiments agreeing on a constant of nature validates the quantum model.

questions.q: How does thermionic emission differ from the photoelectric effect?
options: Thermionic emission needs no energy at all to free electrons
Both must overcome the same work function, but thermionic emission supplies the energy as heat (thermal energy) whereas the photoelectric effect supplies it via individual photons
Thermionic emission frees protons rather than electrons
The photoelectric effect uses heat while thermionic emission uses light
questions.explanation: Both processes must supply at least the work function to free an electron; the difference is the energy source — thermal energy (heat) for thermionic emission, a single photon for the photoelectric effect.

questions.q: Taking diffraction, interference and the photoelectric effect together, what is the correct conclusion about the nature of light?
options: Light is purely a wave; the photoelectric effect must have another explanation
Light is purely a particle; interference is an illusion
Light exhibits wave-particle duality: wave behaviour (diffraction, interference, polarisation) and particle behaviour (photoelectric effect, black-body radiation) are both real
Light is neither a wave nor a particle but a form of heat
questions.explanation: Both bodies of evidence are watertight: you cannot explain a dark interference fringe with particles, nor the threshold frequency with a wave. Light is a quantum object showing wave behaviour in some experiments and particle behaviour in others.
